title: Payroll Services

alternatives.name: Independent payroll service bureau serving small and lower-middle-market employers
inclusion_criteria: An independent operating company whose principal activity is recurring payroll calculation, payroll reporting, direct-deposit administration, and employer tax filing or remittance for business clients.
Owns and bills the employer relationship while using a licensed, white-label, or otherwise third-party core payroll platform.
Falls plausibly within the modeled $1 million to $10 million EBITDA acquisition band after normalizing owner compensation and unsupported add-backs.
Provides payroll services without also principally providing accounting, bookkeeping, or billing services, consistent with the six-digit industry boundary.
exclusion_criteria: Software-first payroll or human-capital-management vendors whose principal economics are proprietary software subscriptions or licenses.
Professional employer organizations, employers of record, and co-employment businesses for which employment-risk transfer or benefits administration is the principal activity.
Payroll-led administrative-services operators whose non-payroll outsourced HR administration is a material part of the customer contract.
CPA, bookkeeping, tax-preparation, or accounting practices that offer payroll only as part of a broader professional-services bundle.
Pure referral agents or resellers that do not control the employer contract or recurring payroll-service revenue.
alternatives.rationale: The six-digit production scope is payroll processing and filing without bundled accounting, while multiple established platform vendors explicitly organize their channels around independent payroll bureaus. The base count is a class-neutral allocation of the modeled 297 target-band firms, not an observed Census cross-tab. It assigns the majority to the service-bureau structure while preserving wide uncertainty for platform ownership and service breadth.

alternatives.name: Payroll-led administrative-services operator without co-employment
inclusion_criteria: Payroll remains the principal service and source of customer relationship control.
The company also contracts for material outsourced HR administration, timekeeping, benefits administration, or compliance support without becoming the common-law or co-employer.
Falls plausibly within the modeled acquisition band.
exclusion_criteria: Pure payroll service bureaus with only incidental ancillary services.
Professional employer organizations, employers of record, staffing firms, and other co-employment models.
Software-first vendors and bundled accounting practices.
alternatives.rationale: Platform partner materials show payroll bureaus expanding into adjacent workforce and HR administration, but the official payroll-services boundary and the exclusion of co-employment models make this a narrower target-band alternative than the pure service-bureau form.

alternatives.name: Proprietary payroll or HCM software vendor selling directly to employers
inclusion_criteria: Owns the core payroll software or platform and contracts directly with employer customers.
Subscription or license economics are principal even when implementation and support services are bundled.
Falls plausibly within the modeled acquisition band and is classified in 541214 despite the software-led delivery model.
exclusion_criteria: Service bureaus licensing or white-labeling another vendor's core payroll engine.
National-scale incumbents above the modeled band.
General-purpose HR software without payroll processing as the principal activity.
alternatives.rationale: Direct proprietary platforms are visible in the market, but the existence of large specialist service-bureau channels and the modeled target-band firm-size distribution support treating software-first vendors as a smaller, economically distinct alternative.

residual.name: Other or unresolved target-band payroll-service structures
residual.rationale: Residual structures include unusual payroll-funding hybrids, firms whose principal revenue boundary cannot be resolved, and classification cases not assignable from public evidence. Bundled accounting practices and co-employment firms remain outside the enumerated 541214 target population rather than entering this residual.

sources.title: 2022 Statistics of U.S. Businesses Annual Data Tables by Establishment Industry
PrismHR platform for PEOs and payroll bureaus
isolved Network partner program for payroll service bureaus
Third party payer arrangements — Payroll service providers and reporting agents
Report on the Payroll Service Provider Industry in New York

target_archetype.selection_basis: The alternatives are mutually exclusive at the company level according to who owns the core platform, who controls and bills the employer contract, and whether material administrative services accompany payroll. Their base counts exhaust the modeled 297 target-band firms when the residual is included: 190 service bureaus, 45 payroll-led administrative-services operators, 35 proprietary software vendors, and 27 unresolved structures. The independent service bureau has the largest base count. The two largest supported ranges do not overlap and their base counts differ by more than ten percent, so the objective overlap rule does not mark the selection uncertain. Counts remain estimates because SUSB does not cross-tab target-band firms by delivery model, platform ownership, or contract form. One selected primary record must be retained if later research challenges the ranges; the industry must not be split into additional records.

value_basis.scope: Employee and contractor cash costs of target-band independent payroll service bureaus

labor_contractor_cash_cost_share.rationale: Use a deliberately broad, class-neutral planning assumption that employee and contractor cash costs consume somewhat less than half of service revenue for a target-band bureau that also pays material third-party platform, payment, insurance, occupancy, and other nonlabor costs. The range allows both highly automated scale operators and high-touch bureaus without claiming an observed industry statistic.
caveats: This is an unsourced feasibility assumption, not a measured target-archetype value.

Cash compensation, payroll taxes, benefits, and contractor fees should be included consistently; platform fees and other vendor costs are excluded unless they purchase embedded labor that the target could actually remove.
The wide range is intentional and forces an UNPROVEN readiness state rather than a source-backed base conclusion.

roles.title: Payroll processing, tax operations, and quality control
roles.rationale: Assumption bucket for recurring data intake, gross-to-net processing, payment preparation, tax filing and remittance operations, reconciliations, and payroll-specific quality control; excludes client support and internal technology administration.

roles.title: Client service, implementation, and issue resolution
roles.rationale: Assumption bucket for onboarding, configuration, training, inbound support, exception resolution, and service remediation; excludes commercial selling and renewal ownership.

roles.title: Sales and commercial account management
roles.rationale: Assumption bucket for lead generation, selling, pricing, renewal management, cross-selling, and commercial relationship ownership; excludes operational support tickets and implementation delivery.

roles.title: Technology, platform administration, security, and data
roles.rationale: Assumption bucket for internal systems, integrations, platform administration, cybersecurity, data operations, and technical vendor management; excludes the third-party platform fee itself.

roles.title: Management, finance, legal, human resources, and administration
roles.rationale: Assumption bucket for executive management, finance, legal and compliance oversight, human resources, and general administration not assigned to the four operating functions.

value_basis.rationale: A bounded assumption is used solely to make the feasibility screen computable without importing the nonrepresentative four-digit NAICS 5412 occupational proxy or claiming target-specific evidence. The five function buckets are mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive cash-cost allocations fixed before research, and their weights sum to one.
caveats: Neither the cash-cost share nor the role weights make a source claim; both carry ASSUMPTION/NONE provenance.
The four-digit occupation basis includes accounting, tax-preparation, and bookkeeping firms that the selected six-digit archetype expressly excludes and is not used.
The research run must preserve the five role identities and weights exactly and may judge only removable fractions within them.
This assumption basis forces UNPROVEN readiness and must not be described as an industry estimate or target observation.

source_hints.source: Current payroll-bureau operating manuals, platform implementation materials, customer contracts, and target-company staffing disclosures
source_hints.why: Needed to separate bureau-performed payroll setup, recurring processing, exception handling, tax filing, and support from work performed by the platform or client.

source_hints.source: Target-archetype client contracts, price schedules, platform reseller agreements, cohort retention disclosures, and comparable private-bureau case studies
source_hints.why: Needed to determine pricing control, revenue retention, platform pass-throughs, and which party captures workflow productivity.

source_hints.source: Closed private payroll-service-bureau transactions, broker sold archives, current BizBuySell, BizQuest, DealStream listings, and ownership-transition evidence
source_hints.why: Needed to estimate the five-year share of selected-archetype firms actually available for purchase; active asks are not completed transactions.

source_hints.source: DealStats, GF Data, PitchBook, Capital IQ, private transaction announcements, and lender underwriting for payroll bureaus
source_hints.why: Needed for private entry and downside-exit multiples with consistent buyer-normalized EBITDA or SDE denominators rather than public software comparables.

source_hints.source: IRS payroll-provider guidance, platform change-of-control terms, private-bureau customer win/loss evidence, and direct-versus-channel platform strategy disclosures
source_hints.why: Needed to isolate paid demand controlled by the bureau from demand controlled by platforms, accountants, banks, benefits brokers, or employer self-service.

implementation: Which payroll setup, recurring processing, tax filing, exception handling, and client-support activities are performed by bureau employees versus the platform vendor or client?
What cash costs can actually be removed or avoided after implementation, net of platform fees, migration, review, compliance, remediation, and customer-service requirements?
What contract, data-migration, tax-account, client-training, and vendor-certification constraints determine the achievable five-year implementation ramp?
commercial_retention: Which recurring fees and ancillary revenues are contractually retained by the bureau, and which economics accrue to the upstream platform, payment processor, benefits partner, or client-funds custodian?
How much freedom does the bureau have to retain productivity gains through margin rather than price reductions, expanded service, higher platform charges, or customer churn?
How do employer size, payroll complexity, service intensity, contract duration, and platform dependence affect the bureau's retained share?
sale_availability: What share of the modeled selected-archetype firms is plausibly available for sale over five years based on completed transactions, active listings, ownership transition evidence, and observed sale processes?
Are client contracts, reporting-agent authorizations, platform licenses, banking relationships, tax accounts, and data rights transferable on a change of control?
How frequently do founder-held client relationships, payroll-funds controls, filing liabilities, cybersecurity exposure, or weak financial records prevent a transaction from closing?
valuation_robustness: What multiples have closed private payroll service bureaus in the relevant earnings band realized on buyer-normalized EBITDA or consistently defined SDE?
How do replacement management compensation, platform migration, required working capital, client attrition, deferred consideration, and assumed liabilities change the all-in entry multiple?

Which private buyer classes acquire scaled payroll service bureaus, and what evidence supports a downside exit multiple for a bureau that remains a services business rather than becoming a software vendor?
Are valuation conclusions robust after excluding public payroll/HCM transactions, live asking prices, client funds, and any interest income unavailable to the target?
operator_survival: What share of year-five paid customer demand remains controlled by the bureau rather than by the upstream platform, accountants, banks, benefits brokers, or employer self-service channels?
Which customer needs require accountable local service, exception resolution, multi-jurisdiction compliance, or relationship continuity that the bureau can continue to own?
Could an upstream platform directly solicit or migrate the bureau's clients, and what contractual or practical protections constrain that risk?
After normalizing founder labor and management replacement, can a target-band bureau operate independently, or is its economic survival dependent on tuck-in consolidation?

special_notes: The archetype enumeration and selection use only industry scope, firm-size structure, platform relationships, and transaction form before any technology-impact evidence is considered.

The dataset n_band value of 297 is an estimate derived from enterprise employment classes, a generic margin, and average revenue per firm; it is not an observed count of firms with $1 million to $10 million EBITDA.
Archetype counts are mutually exclusive estimates whose bases, together with the residual, equal the modeled target-band total; enumeration coverage excludes the residual from the covered share.
Do not equate local bureau branding with ownership of the payroll engine; identify the platform supplier and change-of-control terms wherever possible.
Keep employer operating funds, restricted payroll funds, and interest on client funds separate from ordinary recurring service revenue and transferable excess cash.
Do not mix revenue, recurring-revenue, SDE, seller EBITDA, and buyer-normalized EBITDA multiples, and do not use public payroll-software transactions as direct bureau comparables.
The specification retains one selected primary record under the overlap policy; an unresolved archetype mix widens uncertainty and prevents industry-wide claims rather than creating separate records.
